content:
I think these two don't need to be generic over T, right? Because T is always Self. I.e. they can be

pub trait HasWork<const ID: u64> {
    fn raw_get_work(ptr: *mut Self) -> *mut Work<Self, ID>
}

pub trait HasHrTimer {
    fn raw_get_hr_timer(ptr: *mut Self) -> *mut HrTimer<Self>
}
Well, T could be dyn Trait
I figured that might be the reason, but I haven’t found any documentation describing this.
I did not end up needing it for workqueue, but I do use that feature for the linked list.

Hmm.. but HasSelfPtr<T> is never used as trait bound. ListItem imo is the actual Has* trait here, which describes 1) how to find the field from the struct and 2) how to find the struct from the field.
For example, as long as DTWrap<dyn Foo> impl ListItem<0> here, the linked list API works for it. The fact that DTWrap<dyn Foo> is HasSelftPtr<dyn Foo> is an implementation details.

We can remove HasSelfPtr and the code still works.

diff --git a/rust/kernel/list.rs b/rust/kernel/list.rs
index 8349ff32fc37..a1b8e4088124 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/list.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/list.rs
@@ -13,7 +13,7 @@

 mod impl_list_item_mod;
 pub use self::impl_list_item_mod::{
-    impl_has_list_links, impl_has_list_links_self_ptr, impl_list_item, HasListLinks, HasSelfPtr,
+    impl_has_list_links, impl_has_list_links_self_ptr, impl_list_item, HasListLinks,
 };

 mod arc;
diff --git a/rust/kernel/list/impl_list_item_mod.rs b/rust/kernel/list/impl_list_item_mod.rs
index 202bc6f97c13..10de5036a6ec 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/list/impl_list_item_mod.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/list/impl_list_item_mod.rs
@@ -56,22 +56,6 @@ unsafe fn raw_get_list_links(ptr: *mut Self) -> *mut $crate::list::ListLinks$(<$
 }
 pub use impl_has_list_links;

-/// Declares that the [`ListLinks<ID>`] field in this struct is inside a
-/// [`ListLinksSelfPtr<T, ID>`].
-///
-/// # Safety
-///
-/// The [`ListLinks<ID>`] field of this struct at [`HasListLinks<ID>::raw_get_list_links`] must be
-/// inside a [`ListLinksSelfPtr<T, ID>`].
-///
-/// [`ListLinks<ID>`]: crate::list::ListLinks
-/// [`ListLinksSelfPtr<T, ID>`]: crate::list::ListLinksSelfPtr
-pub unsafe trait HasSelfPtr<T: ?Sized, const ID: u64 = 0>
-where
-    Self: HasListLinks<ID>,
-{
-}
-
 /// Implements the [`HasListLinks`] and [`HasSelfPtr`] traits for the given type.
 #[macro_export]
 macro_rules! impl_has_list_links_self_ptr {
@@ -80,10 +64,6 @@ macro_rules! impl_has_list_links_self_ptr {
        for $self:ty
        { self$(.$field:ident)* }
     )*) => {$(
-        // SAFETY: The implementation of `raw_get_list_links` only compiles if the field has the
-        // right type.
-        unsafe impl$(<$($generics)*>)? $crate::list::HasSelfPtr<$item_type $(, $id)?> for $self {}
-
         unsafe impl$(<$($generics)*>)? $crate::list::HasListLinks$(<$id>)? for $self {
             #[inline]
             unsafe fn raw_get_list_links(ptr: *mut Self) -> *mut $crate::list::ListLinks$(<$id>)? {
We only need to make sure impl_list_item!() use the right implementation.
As far as I can tell, a "field" struct (e.g. Work, HrTimer and ListLink) may need to generic over T, but a "has field" trait seems not.
With regards to HasSelfPtr, it's plausible that it was needed prior to Tamir's changes, but isn't anymore.

Boqun Feng said:

As far as I can tell, a "field" struct (e.g. Work, HrTimer and ListLink) may need to generic over T, but a "has field" trait seems not.

If you have a HasField<Work<T>> trait, then it's still generic over T by virtue of being generic over the field type, or do I misunderstand what you mean?
It’s going to be a trait HasField<F: Field<Self>>, in Work case it’s a HasField<Work<Self>>
Note that even ignoring the dyn Trait case, the type may also mismatch Self if the struct is inside another struct. Binder uses this pretty heavily for the list with DTRWrap
So you may have struct Bar { foo: Foo } plus struct Foo { field: Work<Bar> }.
of course the impl that matters is HasWork<Bar> for Bar
but Foo should NOT HasField<Work<Foo>> in this case, right?
Right, if anything it is Foo impl HasField<Work<Bar>>
or just Bar impl HasField<Work<Bar>>?
sure
but e.g. ListArcSafe performs the trait recursively when it comes to the tracked by stuff
So DTRWrap<T> is ListArcSafe with tracking by whatever the wrapped field uses
and each T used with it then implements ListArcSafe with some mechanism
So DTRWrap<Transaction> implements ListArcSafe with untracked tracking. Whereas DTRWrap<Node> implements it with an AtomicTracker inside struct Node
well, maybe ListArcSafe is a bit of a different concept - it's not really an HasField
Yeah
also ListArcSafe is not generic over T as well.
yeah I realize now
there's no need to perform container_of from the field to the struct
that's what the T is for - it's the return type of container_of
so if there's no container_of, there's no need for the T
but even with container_of, we can use Self, right? Because we don't do impl HasField<F: Field<Self>> for dyn Foo.
i.e. we always impl HasField<...> for a Sized type.
Binder implements HasListLinks<DTRWrap<dyn DeliverToRead>>
for DTRWrap<dyn DeliverToRead>

Boqun Feng said:

i.e. we always impl HasField<...> for a Sized type.

Oh, this is not correct
We do, but we just use more than container_of
It implements container_of not by a pointer offset, but by storing a pointer to the original struct.
next to the prev/next pointers
well
the offset is actually fixed, so we could get away with only storing the vtable

Alice Ryhl said:

Binder implements HasListLinks<DTRWrap<dyn DeliverToRead>>

HasListLinks is not generic over T though
What we actually did was: impl HasListLink<ID> for DTRWrap<dyn DeliverToRead>.
oh
and view_value() uses Self as the type of the container
I remember now. Work<T> is generic because Work::new needs to know what the surrounding struct is to construct the function pointer stored within it.

Alice Ryhl said:

I remember now. Work<T> is generic because Work::new needs to know what the surrounding struct is to construct the function pointer stored within it.

Right, the Field type needs to be generic over the Container type.
but HasField trait may not need to generic over the Container type.
But with the workqueue, Work<Foo> and Work<Bar> may be present in the same linked list. That never happens with List.
That's true.
By the way, HasWork is just a utility to help you implement the real trait RawWorkItem
well I guess ListItem is the same situation
And that's another hint for me, because RawWorkItem and ListItem are not generic, which may suggest the generic part is not needed.
Anyway, I probably keep a trait HasField<T, F: Field<T>> at the moment, and see if I could replace all Has* trait and then see if I could get rid of the generic T part.
sounds sensible
My fear is also removing T could restrict some usages that we haven't forseen, and keeping it is not that bad ;-) But let's see.
Mainly I would make sure to think about the implications towards nested structs and for trait objects.
both are used by Binder for List
so you have a test case
For sure

here it's relevant to consider both fixed offsets: DTRWrap<dyn DeliverToRead> has ListLinks<0> at a fixed offset, but the inner type may have a ListLinks<1> at a dynamic offset (though it's used with List<DTRWrap<SpecificStruct>> in that case)
I believe List<dyn Trait> requires a fixed offset
whereas workqueue obviously supports dynamic offsets
I also need to make sure a HasField<DelayedWork<T>> can implies HasField<Work<T>>, which is another interesting requirement.
yes, and same for ListLinksSelfPtr
Thanks! Going to poke the code a bit tomorrow and see what I get.
:thumbs_up:
